title: How to Work with Shame and Self-Judgment During Psychedelic Journeys
body:
How to Work with Shame and Self-Judgment During Psychedelic Journeys

1. Opening Hook - The Quiet Battle Within

Have you ever sat alone with your thoughts during a psychedelic journey, feeling exposed and raw, only to be haunted by the relentless echo of shame and self-judgment? I’ve been there. More times than I can count. One night, deep into a psilocybin session, I found myself trapped in a loop of self-recrimination. Thoughts like “I’m not enough,” “I’m a failure,” and “I don’t deserve healing” crawled through my mind, each one heavier than the last. It felt like drowning in quicksand made of my own insecurities.

Shame is a sneaky beast. Unlike guilt, which points to specific actions, shame targets who we are. During psychedelic journeys, those hidden corners of shame often see the light of day, amplified by the mind’s unfiltered state. It’s not unusual to feel overwhelmed, disconnected, or even paralyzed when shame surfaces. In fact, shame is one of the most common emotional blocks I’ve witnessed in my years of both personal experience and working alongside others in the psychedelic space.

What’s fascinating - and terrifying - is how psychedelics can crack open these walls we’ve built around shame. Sometimes the experience feels like a confrontation, a forced reckoning with parts of ourselves we’d rather bury. And while that can be painful, it can also be the beginning of something deeply healing. But only if you know how to work with shame, rather than run from it or fight it.

In this article, I want to share what I’ve learned about the relationship between psychedelic journeys and shame. We’ll talk about why shame surfaces, how science explains this phenomenon, and practical ways to move through those rough waters. You won’t be alone in this. There’s a path through, even when it feels like shame has the upper hand. And sometimes, psychedelics offer a unique opportunity to rewrite the story you tell yourself about who you are.

2. Understanding Shame and Self-Judgment in the Psychedelic Context

Shame lives in a different place than your everyday thoughts. It’s wired deep into our brains as a social and emotional regulator - a tool that evolved to keep us connected to others by discouraging behaviors that might lead to rejection. But when shame becomes chronic, it morphs into a toxic force that eats away at our self-worth.

During a psychedelic experience, especially with substances like psilocybin, LSD, or MDMA, the brain undergoes significant shifts in activity and connectivity. Research led by scientists like Robin Carhart-Harris has shown how psychedelics quiet the default mode network (DMN), a set of brain regions involved in self-referential thinking and what we often call the "ego." The DMN is where much of our narrative self lives, including patterns of negative self-talk and internalized shame.

With the DMN dampened, those rigid narratives loosen, and you get a glimpse of yourself beyond the usual mental filters. This is why some people describe their trips as moments of clarity or rebirth. But here’s the catch: when shame is deeply ingrained, it doesn’t just disappear. Instead, it can surface vividly, stripped from the usual mental defenses that keep it hidden. This exposure can feel like emotional stripping, leaving you vulnerable to all those harsh judgments you usually keep buried.

From a neuroscience perspective, shame activates areas like the anterior cingulate cortex and insula, which are linked to processing pain and negative emotion. Psychedelics alter activity in these regions, making the experience of shame feel both magnified and, paradoxically, more malleable. The intensity can be overwhelming, but it also means these painful feelings are accessible to conscious awareness and potential healing.

What’s more, shame often ties into trauma, as Bessel van der Kolk explores in The Body Keeps the Score. Traumatic experiences can leave imprints of shame that color your self-perception for years. Psychedelics, by loosening cognitive and emotional rigidity, can peel back these layers to reveal the roots of shame. That’s why integrating trauma work into psychedelic therapy is so critical.

3. Research and Evidence: What the Science Says About Shame and Psychedelics

Scientific exploration of shame during psychedelic experiences is still emerging, but several key studies shed light on this dynamic. For starters, research by Roland Griffiths at Johns Hopkins University has demonstrated how psilocybin can lead to lasting changes in personality traits like openness and reduce symptoms of depression and anxiety, conditions often intertwined with shame.

One particularly revealing study published in the Journal of Psychopharmacology focused on how psychedelic-assisted therapy impacts self-criticism and shame. Researchers found that after sessions with psilocybin, participants reported substantially reduced feelings of shame - sometimes described as "a softening" of the internal critic. Importantly, this wasn’t just anecdotal. Quantitative measures taken before and after sessions showed significant decreases in shame-related symptoms, with effects lasting months.

The work of Michael Pollan, chronicled in his book How to Change Your Mind, offers vivid descriptions of how psychedelics can disrupt old narratives. Pollan highlights how this disruption can unearth shame but also create the conditions to reevaluate it from a new perspective, distancing the self from harsh judgments.

Further, research on MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD, an area pioneered by organizations like MAPS, shows that reducing fear responses allows patients to revisit traumatic memories without the overwhelming shame that usually blocks healing. This work suggests that psychedelics can act as a kind of "emotional reset," making shame feel less like a fixed identity and more like a passing experience.

Stanislav Grof’s early explorations into psychedelic therapy also provide a structure for understanding these sessions. He described how confronting painful feelings, including shame, is often a necessary step towards "spiritual rebirth." Grof’s approach suggests that shame, when faced openly in a supported setting, can transform from a barrier into a doorway.

While the research is promising, it’s clear that psychedelics don’t erase shame outright. Rather, they create a unique state where shame can be examined, challenged, and ultimately softened with proper support and integration.

4. Practical Application: How to Work with Shame and Self-Judgment During Your Journey

Knowing that shame can surface powerfully during psychedelic sessions is one thing. Actually handling it in the moment is another challenge altogether. Here’s what I’ve learned about working with these emotions without getting stuck.

First, preparation is key. Before your journey, spend time setting an intention around self-compassion. Remind yourself that shame is a feeling, not a fact. It’s a voice, not the whole story. Writing this intention down in a journal can help. If you want a sturdy companion for this practice, something like the Journaling Notebook Leather Bound offers a tactile way to track your thoughts and emotions before, during, and after your trip.

During the experience, try to adopt an attitude of curiosity rather than resistance. When shame pops up, instead of pushing it away, ask yourself, “What is this shame trying to tell me?” and “Where in my body do I feel this?” Studies suggest that naming emotions and linking them to bodily sensations can reduce their intensity. The body holds onto shame tightly, as van der Kolk reveals in The Body Keeps the Score. Bringing gentle attention to your body can ground you and make the experience feel less like drowning.

If the shame becomes overwhelming, grounding techniques are a lifeline. Simple breathwork or focusing on the texture of an object in your hand can help you stay present. Having a trusted sitter or guide who understands the emotional terrain can also be invaluable, offering reassurance when your inner critic gets loud.

Remember, you don’t have to make the shame disappear in the moment. Sometimes, just sitting with it, witnessing it without judgment, makes it less powerful. Psychedelic experiences often bring heightened emotional sensitivity, and holding space for these feelings can be more healing than trying to fix or fight them.

Finally, post-session reflection is vital. Writing about your feelings, even the difficult ones, helps solidify new insights. This is where a good journal comes into play again. Integration sessions with a therapist or peer group familiar with psychedelic experiences can provide structure and support, helping you untangle shame’s grip long after the trip ends.

5. Common Challenges When Facing Shame on Psychedelics and How to Work Through Them

Facing shame during a psychedelic journey is rarely straightforward. There are common stumbling blocks that can make the process feel harder than it needs to be.

One of the biggest challenges is the flood of overwhelming emotion. When shame surfaces, it can hijack your nervous system, triggering fight, flight, or freeze responses. This can lead to panic, withdrawal, or even a bad trip. Recognizing this as the body’s survival mechanism, rather than a sign you’re “failing” at the journey, can reduce secondary shame about feeling overwhelmed.

Another challenge is the tendency to dissociate, especially if shame is tied to past trauma. Dissociation is a protective mechanism your brain uses to shut down unbearable feelings. While it can feel like an escape, it also disconnects you from the opportunity to heal the shame. Gentle grounding techniques - like placing your feet on the ground or feeling your heartbeat - can help pull you back to the present moment.

Sometimes, shame hides in the form of self-criticism during the trip: “I’m not strong enough to handle this,” or “I’m broken.” These thoughts can spin out of control, but recognizing them as just thoughts - not facts - can weaken their hold. Mindfulness practices that focus on observing thoughts without attachment are helpful here.

Resistance is another common hurdle. It’s natural to want to suppress shame because it hurts, but resistance often deepens the suffering. I encourage people to try the opposite: lean into the discomfort with kindness. It’s like holding a fragile bird - you don’t squeeze it tight; you offer gentle support until it can fly again.

Finally, shame can be complicated by the fear of judgment from others after the journey. Opening up about your experience might feel risky, especially if it includes painful admissions. Curating a safe circle of friends, therapists, or psychedelic integration groups can provide a judgment-free zone to unpack your feelings.

Remember, these challenges are not signs of weakness or failure. They’re part of the process. Every moment you face shame with courage is a step toward reclaiming your sense of self beyond judgment.

6. Integration with Daily Life: Maintaining Compassion Beyond the Trip

The psychedelic session might be a few hours or days, but the work with shame stretches far beyond that. Integration - the steady work of weaving insights into your everyday life - is where lasting change happens.

One of the best tools for integration is journaling. Writing down your experiences, especially moments when shame surfaced and how you responded, builds awareness and self-compassion over time. I keep a leather-bound journal for this very purpose because there’s something grounding about putting pen to paper that digital notes can’t replicate. If you want to try this too, check out this Journaling Notebook Leather Bound.

Bringing mindfulness into your daily routine can shift how you relate to shame. Practices like meditation or mindful breathing remind you that emotions are temporary and don’t define who you are. Some people find body-based practices like yoga or somatic therapy particularly helpful since shame is often stored in the body, as van der Kolk details extensively.

Therapeutic support is another pillar. Working with a therapist familiar with psychedelic experiences, trauma, and shame can provide a container for exploring difficult emotions safely. Integration sessions aren’t just about recounting the trip; they’re about rewiring your relationship with self-judgment over time.

Another powerful practice is cultivating self-compassion. Kristin Neff’s research on self-compassion shows how treating yourself kindly can reduce shame and increase emotional resilience. You don’t have to wait for a crisis or another psychedelic session to practice this. Simple daily affirmations or moments of kindness toward yourself can chip away at shame’s hold.

Finally, connecting with others who understand your journey can help normalize shame and reduce isolation. Integration circles, peer groups, or even informal conversations with trusted friends create community. Shame thrives in secrecy but loses power when brought into the light.

7. Closing Reflection - Meeting Shame as a Teacher, Not an Enemy

As I reflect on the long relationship I’ve had with shame - both in and out of psychedelic journeys - I realize it’s not an enemy to be destroyed but a messenger to be heard. Shame signals places where we’ve been hurt, misunderstood, or disconnected from ourselves and others. When psychedelics crack open these wounds, the rawness can feel unbearable, yet it also offers a rare invitation to rewrite old stories.

Consider shame like a shadow following you in the dark. Most of us try to outrun it, but the more you run, the louder it seems. Instead, what if you stopped, turned around, and greeted that shadow with curiosity? What would happen if you held it gently instead of fighting it? Psychedelic journeys often place us in this exact situation - a confrontation with shadows we usually avoid.

This is where the real work begins. The courage to look at shame, to acknowledge it without blinking away, can open a space between the feeling and your sense of self. You start to see that shame is not who you are. It’s a part of your story but not the author of it.

Here’s the radical truth I’ve found: shame thrives in silence and secrecy, but when you bring it into consciousness, especially in the altered states psychedelics offer, it loses the power to control you. That doesn’t mean shame vanishes overnight or becomes easy to face. It means you develop a relationship with it - a dance where you lead, not the other way around.

So next time shame rises during your journey, try to remember that it’s a teacher wearing a rough mask. Listen to what it’s saying beneath the harsh words. And when you come back to the everyday world, carry with you the knowledge that healing is not about being perfect or without flaws. It’s about meeting yourself fully, even the parts you’d rather hide.

That’s where freedom starts.
